metadata.summary: Patterns for using reasoning models (o3/o4-mini, Claude Extended Thinking, DeepSeek R1) and chain-of-thought techniques to solve tasks requiring multi-step logic, self-verification, and planning.

content.title: Reasoning-First Architectures
content.summary: Patterns for using reasoning models (o3/o4-mini, Claude Extended Thinking, DeepSeek R1) and chain-of-thought techniques to solve tasks requiring multi-step logic, self-verification, and planning. Route by task complexity: use cheap classifiers to decide when a reasoning model is warranted, set thinking budgets explicitly, and always gate irreversible downstream actions behind human review.

why.p: Reasoning models scale test-time compute to achieve breakthrough accuracy on hard tasks: o3 scores 83.3% on AIME 2024 vs 13.4% for GPT-4o. The cost is 10-50x per call. The key insight is that not all tasks need deep reasoning — routing only complex tasks to reasoning models while using cheap models for simple ones keeps cost under control while unlocking qualitatively better outputs for the tasks that matter.

list.item: Multi-step math, logic, or formal proofs where intermediate steps matter
Code generation requiring self-verification before returning
Research synthesis where competing hypotheses must be explored before concluding
Planning tasks with dependencies where ordering must be validated
Any workflow where the cost of a wrong answer outweighs the cost of extra tokens
Simple retrieval or lookup tasks — CoT adds latency with no quality gain
High-throughput classification or routing (thousands of calls per minute)
Creative writing where deliberate reasoning constrains output quality
Cost-sensitive pipelines where standard models already meet the bar (verified by eval)
Real-time streaming where users see partial output — reasoning tokens break UX

rule.statement: Use o4-mini for cost-effective reasoning tasks (math, code review, logic). Use Claude Extended Thinking when long context (200K+) or Anthropic API integration is required. Use DeepSeek R1 for budget-critical offline batch tasks.
rule.rationale: o3 and Claude 4 Opus are 10-50x more expensive than standard models. o4-mini at $1.10/M input with reasoning provides 83% of o3 quality at 55% of the cost. DeepSeek R1 at $0.55/M input is the cheapest option with visible reasoning tokens.

list.item: o3: $2.00/M input, $8.00/M output + reasoning billed as output, 200K context, private CoT
o4-mini: $1.10/M input, $4.40/M output, 200K context, private CoT
Claude 4 Opus: $15.00/M input, $75.00/M output, 200K context, Extended Thinking
Claude 4 Sonnet: $3.00/M input, $15.00/M output, Extended Thinking
DeepSeek R1: $0.55/M input, $2.19/M output, 128K context, visible think tags

section.name: Performance Benchmarks
section.p: Key benchmarks showing where reasoning models excel over standard models:

list.item: AIME 2024 (math): o3=83.3%, DeepSeek R1=79.8%, Claude 4 ET=75.0%, GPT-4o=13.4%
GPQA Diamond (science): o3=87.7%, Claude 4 ET=84.8%, DeepSeek R1=71.5%, GPT-4o=53.6%
SWE-bench (code): o3=71.7%, Claude 4 ET=72.5%, DeepSeek R1=49.2%, GPT-4o=33.2%
HumanEval (code): GPT-4o=90.2%, o3=92.4%, Claude 4 ET=89.0%

rule.statement: Always verify that a reasoning model outperforms a standard model on your specific task before committing to reasoning model costs. Run eval on 50-100 representative examples first.
rule.rationale: Reasoning models excel at formal logic, math, and code with edge cases. For tasks like creative writing, summarization, or simple retrieval, standard models often match quality at 10-50x lower cost per call.

rule.statement: Set thinking budget by task type: 1K-4K tokens for formatting/extraction, 4K-8K for code review and logic, 8K-32K for architecture planning, 32K-128K for deep research and theorem proving.
rule.rationale: Thinking budget is an upper bound — the model decides how much to use. Setting it too low truncates reasoning on complex tasks; setting it too high wastes cost on simple tasks. Empirically calibrate budgets per task type using eval data.

example.title: Task-type budget selector

rule.statement: Use ReAct (Reason + Act) for tool-using agents that need to explain their steps. Use Reflexion when the agent needs to self-evaluate and improve its output across multiple iterations. Use Tree-of-Thought only for problems with multiple valid solution paths.
rule.rationale: ReAct makes agent reasoning transparent and auditable. Reflexion is cost-effective for quality-critical outputs: generate → critique → improve loop is cheaper than a single expensive reasoning model call. Tree-of-Thought explores branches and is only justified when the search space has genuinely different solution paths.

list.item: ReAct: interleaved reasoning and tool use; makes steps auditable
Reflexion: generate → self-critique → improve; good for code and writing
Tree-of-Thought: explore multiple paths, prune and select; complex problem solving
Planning Loop: plan → execute → verify → adjust; multi-step task execution
Critique and Revise: generate → expert critique → revision; quality-critical outputs

rule.statement: Use a cheap model (haiku/gpt-4o-mini) to classify task complexity before dispatching to a reasoning model. Gate reasoning model invocation behind a classifier that evaluates whether the task requires multi-step verification.
rule.rationale: Sending all tasks to reasoning models wastes 10-50x per call. A fast classifier adds 10-30ms and costs fractions of a cent. Most production traffic is simple tasks that don't benefit from extended reasoning.

example.title: Complexity-based routing
example.code:
import anthropic client = anthropic.Anthropic()
def route_task(task: str) -> str: """Classify complexity and return recommended model.""" classifier_response = client.messages.create( model="claude-haiku-4-5", max_tokens=20, messages=[{"role": "user", "content": f"Rate task complexity: simple/complex. Task: {task[:200]}"}] ) if "complex" in classifier_response.content[0].text.lower(): return "claude-opus-4-5" # reasoning model return "claude-haiku-4-5" # fast model

rule.statement: Set max_tokens high enough to accommodate thinking tokens plus the answer. If max_tokens is too low, thinking is truncated and the response may be incomplete. Budget: thinking_budget + expected_output_tokens + 1000 buffer.
rule.rationale: Extended Thinking uses tokens from max_tokens budget. A common mistake is setting max_tokens=4096 with budget_tokens=8192 — the model tries to think 8K tokens but max_tokens caps the total, truncating both thinking and answer.

example.title: Correct max_tokens sizing
example.code:
import anthropic client = anthropic.Anthropic()
budget = 8000 # thinking tokens response = client.messages.create( model="claude-opus-4-5", max_tokens=budget + 4000, # thinking + answer buffer thinking={"type": "enabled", "budget_tokens": budget}, messages=[{"role": "user", "content": task}] ) # content[0] is ThinkingBlock, content[1] is TextBlock answer = response.content[1].text

rule.statement: Add per-call cost guards in agent loops: o3 with reasoning_effort=high can cost $5-15 per call. Set a hard budget per reasoning call and abort if the estimated token count exceeds it.
rule.rationale: Reasoning tokens are billed as output tokens. A single reasoning call with a large thinking budget and complex task can consume the monthly budget of a small team. Cost guards prevent runaway spending in automated agent loops.

rule.statement: Cache reasoning outputs for identical inputs using content hash as the key. Reasoning tokens are non-deterministic even at temperature=0 — cache the final answer, not the reasoning trace.
rule.rationale: Reasoning calls are expensive. Identical tasks in agent loops (same planning step, same verification) should not re-invoke the reasoning model. Cache the final answer by input hash; discard the reasoning trace after extracting the answer.

rule.statement: Gate any irreversible action (deploy, delete, send, payment) that follows from reasoning output behind a human review checkpoint. Reasoning models can produce confident-sounding but incorrect reasoning chains.
rule.rationale: Extended thinking is not verified reasoning — it is a prediction of what reasoning would look like. Models hallucinate confidently-sounding logic chains just as they hallucinate facts. Human review prevents automated execution of plausible-but-wrong reasoning.

list.item: Claude Extended Thinking: cannot be combined with system prompt caching in the same request (as of 2026-01); check API changelog
Claude Extended Thinking: cannot be interrupted mid-thought; no streaming of reasoning tokens to the user; set timeout >120s in agent harness
o3/o4 tool use: batch tool calls per reasoning step to reduce round-trips; interleaved thinking with tool calls has higher latency per tool
DeepSeek R1: think blocks may contain PII echoed from input — scrub before logging
o3/o4: reasoning tokens are non-deterministic even at temperature=0; don't diff raw reasoning between runs

antipattern.description: Using reasoning models for high-throughput classification (thousands of calls per minute).
antipattern.reason: Reasoning models have higher latency (5-30s per call) and cost (10-50x). At high throughput, reasoning cost becomes prohibitive and latency makes the system unusable. Use standard models with classification prompts; escalate to reasoning models only for edge cases flagged by the classifier.

antipattern.description: Streaming DeepSeek R1 think tags directly to users.
antipattern.reason: DeepSeek R1 think tags contain the model's internal monologue including potentially confidential input echoing and intermediate reasoning that should not be visible to end users. Always strip <think> blocks before returning output.

section.name: Agentic Workflow
section.p: A subagent receives a complex task (planning, debugging, theorem proving). It invokes a reasoning model with a tuned thinking budget — low budget (1K-4K tokens) for structural tasks, high budget (32K-128K) for math/code. The reasoning output is then passed to a cheaper model for summarization or formatting. Human review checkpoints should gate any destructive downstream actions that follow from the reasoning.
section.note: Recommended subagents: faion-sdd-executor-agent (wraps reasoning calls for architecture design and spec validation tasks), nero-sdd-executor-agent (same pattern for NERO-internal planning loops)

rule.statement: Use OpenAI API (o3, o4-mini) for production reasoning tasks with visible token usage. Use Anthropic API (Claude Extended Thinking) when long context or API integration is required. Use DeepSeek API for batch/offline reasoning on budget.
rule.rationale: Each provider offers different tradeoffs: OpenAI leads in pure reasoning performance but is most expensive; Claude offers longest context and best API; DeepSeek is cheapest but visible reasoning tokens require sanitization.

list.item: openai Python SDK: Call o3/o4-mini with reasoning_effort param (pip install openai / platform.openai.com)
anthropic Python SDK: Claude Extended Thinking via thinking= param (pip install anthropic / docs.anthropic.com)
litellm: Unified interface: switch models without code changes (pip install litellm / litellm.ai)
promptfoo: Eval harness to compare reasoning vs standard models (npx promptfoo / promptfoo.dev)
OpenAI API (o3, o4-mini): SaaS; reasoning_effort: low/medium/high; returns usage.reasoning_tokens
Anthropic API (Claude 3.7/4): SaaS; thinking.budget_tokens 1K-128K; streaming supported for text block
DeepSeek API (R1): SaaS; Returns <think> block in content; cheap ($0.55/M input)
Replicate (QwQ-32B): SaaS; Open-weight; good for batch offline reasoning
Together AI: SaaS; Hosts DeepSeek R1, QwQ; supports streaming
Ollama (local): OSS; QwQ-32B, DeepSeek-R1-distilled; no API key; limited context
Always measure: run eval on a sample before committing to a reasoning model in production
Route by task: use a cheap model to classify task complexity, then dispatch to reasoning model only when warranted
Set max_tokens high enough to accommodate reasoning tokens + answer; undershoot causes truncated thinking
For Claude: budget_tokens does not guarantee usage — model decides; set it as an upper bound
Strip <think> blocks before passing DeepSeek R1 output downstream or to users
Cache reasoning outputs for identical inputs (same hash) to avoid re-billing reasoning tokens
When using o3 tool use, batch tool calls per reasoning step to reduce round-trips
Reasoning tokens are non-deterministic even with temperature=0 on some providers; don't compare raw reasoning text between runs
Extended Thinking cannot be used with system prompt caching on Claude in the same request (as of 2026-01); check API changelog
reasoning_effort=high on o3 can spike a single call to $5-15; add per-call cost guards in agent loops
Human-in-loop checkpoint required before any irreversible action (deploy, delete, send) that follows from reasoning output
DeepSeek R1 <think> blocks may contain PII echoed from input — scrub before logging
Tool calls interleaved with Extended Thinking (Claude) extend latency nonlinearly; set timeout > 120s in agent harness

template.purpose: Claude Extended Thinking minimal invocation with budget selector function
System prompt pattern for careful reasoning agents with verification steps
